content.text: What is Self-Service Password Reset?

Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) lets users reset their own Entra ID passwords without contacting the help desk. Users verify their identity using pre-registered methods (authenticator app, email, SMS, security questions), then set a new password. The reset can optionally be written back to on-premises Active Directory via Password Writeback.

SSPR reduces help desk call volume — password reset requests typically account for 20–50% of IT support tickets. By delegating password reset to users themselves, organizations reclaim engineer time while keeping accounts secure through identity verification.

attrs.alt: SSPR flow diagram showing user registration, identity verification methods, password reset, and optional writeback to on-premises AD

content.text: SSPR Registration and Method Requirements

Users must register authentication methods before they can use SSPR. Registration happens at aka.ms/ssprsetup, or users are prompted during sign-in via Combined Security Information Registration. The policy controls how many methods must be registered and which are allowed.

# Monitor SSPR registration completeness
# Users must pre-register auth methods before they can use SSPR

# Get registration details for all users
$registrations = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/reports/authenticationMethods/userRegistrationDetails" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

$total  = $registrations.Count
$ready  = ($registrations | Where-Object { $_.isSsprRegistered -eq $true }).Count
$capable = ($registrations | Where-Object { $_.isSsprCapable -eq $true }).Count

Write-Host "SSPR Registration Report"
Write-Host "Total users: $total"
Write-Host "Registered: $ready ($([math]::Round($ready/$total*100))%)"
Write-Host "Capable (enough methods): $capable ($([math]::Round($capable/$total*100))%)"

# Find users NOT yet registered (need to be nudged)
$unregistered = $registrations | Where-Object { $_.isSsprRegistered -eq $false }
$unregistered | Select-Object userPrincipalName, isAdmin, isMfaRegistered |
    Export-Csv -Path C:\sspr-unregistered.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Enforce registration at next sign-in via Conditional Access
# (Entra ID will prompt users to complete Combined Security Info Registration)
Method
SSPR Support
MFA Support
Phishing Resistant
Notes
Microsoft Authenticator (notification)
Yes
Yes
Partial
Preferred — strongest usability balance
Microsoft Authenticator (OTP)
Yes
Yes
No
OTP from app without push
Email OTP (alternate email)
Yes
No
No
External email only, not work email
SMS / phone call
Yes
Yes
No
Carrier-dependent; SIM-swap risk
Security questions
Yes
No
No
Weakest — consider disabling for privileged accounts
FIDO2 security keys
No
Yes
Yes
Not supported for SSPR as of 2025

Combined Registration (aka.ms/mysecurityinfo) lets users register both MFA and SSPR methods in a single flow. Enable it to avoid duplicate registration prompts and ensure method consistency across both features.
Enabling SSPR and Configuring the Policy

SSPR can be scoped to all users, a selected group (for phased rollout), or disabled. The policy also controls the number of required verification methods (1 or 2), which methods are allowed, and whether admins are excluded from certain weaker methods.

# Enable SSPR via Microsoft Graph
# Requires: Policy.ReadWrite.AuthenticationMethod permission

# Enable SSPR for all users (set to 'all', 'selected', or 'none')
$params = @{
    selfServiceSignUp = @{ isEnabled = $true }
}
# SSPR enablement is in passwordResetPolicies
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authorizationPolicy" \
    -Body (@{ allowInvitesFrom = "none" } | ConvertTo-Json)  # illustrative patch endpoint

# Preferred: Use the Entra admin center or the directory settings API
# GET current policy:
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/adminConsentRequestPolicy"

# Enable SSPR for a specific group via directory settings:
$groupId = "<target-group-object-id>"
$settings = @{
    templateId = "5cf42378-d67d-4f36-ba46-e8b86229381d"  # PasswordReset template
    values = @(
        @{ name = "SsprEnabled"; value = "true" },
        @{ name = "SsprEnabledGroup"; value = $groupId },
        @{ name = "NumberOfMethodsRequired"; value = "1" },
        @{ name = "EnabledForResetByAdmins"; value = "false" }
    )
}
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/settings" \
    -Body ($settings | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 4)
NumberOfMethodsRequired: 1 reduces friction but means a single compromised method allows reset; 2 is stronger
Admin accounts: admins are always required to use 2 methods and cannot use security questions for SSPR
Phased rollout: start with a pilot group (e.g., IT department), then expand after validating registration rates
Notify on reset: configure Entra ID to send email to the user and optionally to a secondary address when their password is reset via SSPR
Prevent SSPR from bypassing MFA: if a user resets their password, require them to satisfy MFA at next sign-in (enable 'Require users to re-register' setting)
Password Writeback to On-Premises Active Directory

For hybrid environments using Entra Connect (formerly Azure AD Connect), Password Writeback synchronizes SSPR password changes to on-premises AD DS in near real-time (typically under 2 minutes). Without writeback, cloud-reset passwords diverge from the on-premises credential, locking hybrid users out of local resources.

# Enable Password Writeback in Entra Connect
# Run on the Entra Connect server

# Check if writeback is currently enabled:
Get-ADSyncConnector | Where-Object { $_.Type -eq "Windows Azure Active Directory (Microsoft)" } |
    Select-Object Name, @{N="PasswordWriteback";E={$_.GlobalParameters["Microsoft.InformationProtection.Fci.FciEnabled"]}}

# Enable writeback via Set-ADSyncPasswordWritebackConfiguration
Import-Module ADSync
Set-ADSyncPasswordWritebackConfiguration -Enable $true
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta

# Verify writeback is working — check Entra Connect event logs
$events = Get-WinEvent -LogName "Application" -ComputerName $env:COMPUTERNAME |
    Where-Object {
        $_.ProviderName -eq "ADSync" -and
        $_.Id -in @(6329, 6329, 31001, 31002, 31006, 31007)
    } | Select-Object TimeCreated, Id, Message -First 20
$events | Format-List

# Required AD DS permissions for the Entra Connect service account:
# - Reset Password on user objects (inheritable)
# - Write lockoutTime on user objects
# - Write pwdLastSet on user objects
# Grant via AD Delegation Control Wizard or dsacls:
dsacls "DC=contoso,DC=com" /I:S /G "CONTOSO\ADConnectSvc:CA;Reset Password;user"
Scenario
Writeback Required?
Behavior Without Writeback
Cloud-only users
No
Reset works; no on-prem account to sync
Hybrid users (synced from AD)
Yes
Cloud password resets — but next PHS sync overwrites it back
Federated users (AD FS)
Yes (+ AD FS config)
Cloud reset blocked unless Entra Connect configured
Password Hash Sync (PHS)
Yes for SSPR to persist
Hash sync overwrites cloud password within sync cycle

Writeback encrypts the new password using the on-premises AD DS public key before sending it over the service bus. Entra Connect decrypts and applies it to AD. The cloud never stores the plaintext password.
Auditing and Monitoring SSPR Events

SSPR activities are logged in Entra ID audit logs under the 'SelfServicePasswordManagement' category. Monitor failed attempts, incomplete flows, and geographic anomalies. Alerting on repeated failed SSPR attempts can detect account takeover probing.

# Audit SSPR events via Microsoft Graph Activity Logs
# SSPR events appear in auditLogs/directoryAudits with category = SelfServicePasswordManagement

$filter = "?" + [System.Web.HttpUtility]::UrlEncode(
    "$filter=category eq 'SelfServicePasswordManagement'&$top=100"
)
$auditLogs = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/directoryAudits$filter" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

$auditLogs | Select-Object ` 
    activityDateTime,
    activityDisplayName,   # e.g. "Self-service password reset flow activity progress"
    result,               # success, failure
    @{N="User";E={$_.targetResources[0].userPrincipalName}},
    @{N="Detail";E={$_.additionalDetails | Where-Object key -eq 'ResetPipelineStage' | Select-Object -ExpandProperty value}}

# Common activityDisplayName values:
# "Self-service password reset flow activity progress"  – each SSPR step
# "User successfully reset their password"              – completed reset
# "User failed to reset their password"                 – failed attempt

# Alert on failed SSPR attempts from unusual locations (sign-in risk)
$failed = $auditLogs | Where-Object result -eq "failure"
Write-Host "Failed SSPR attempts (last 100 events): $($failed.Count)"
Export SSPR reports from the Entra admin center: Azure AD > Password reset > Audit logs
Integrate audit logs with Microsoft Sentinel for automated alerting on anomalous SSPR patterns
Track the 'SSPR Capable' percentage in your tenant monthly — target >90% of enabled users registered
Correlate SSPR successes with subsequent impossible-travel sign-ins to detect account compromise via SSPR
Configuring the SSPR Policy via Microsoft Graph

The SSPR policy controls which users can reset, how many methods are required, which methods are allowed, and helpdesk customization. The Graph beta endpoint `policies/passwordResetPolicies` exposes the full policy object. Changes take effect within a few minutes of the PATCH call.

# Comprehensive SSPR policy configuration via Graph API (beta)
# Scopes: Policy.ReadWrite.AuthenticationMethod

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Policy.ReadWrite.AuthenticationMethod"

# Get the SSPR policy (passwordResetPolicies)
$policy = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/passwordResetPolicies"
$policy | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 6

# Update: enable SSPR for selected group with 2 methods required
$pilotGroupId = "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001"  # replace with real group ID
$updateBody = @{
    selfServicePasswordResetEnabledType = "selected"   # 'all', 'selected', 'none'
    enabledGroupIds = @($pilotGroupId)
    registrationRequireReregistrationAfterDays = 180    # re-register every 180 days
    registrationShouldBlockSignIn = $false              # don't block sign-in if not registered
    notifyUsersOnPasswordReset = $true                  # email user when password is reset
    notifyOnAdminPasswordReset = $true                  # email admins when admin pwd is reset
    numberOfMethodsRequired = 2                         # require 2 methods for reset
    allowedMethods = @(
        "email",           # alternate email
        "mobilePhone",     # SMS to mobile number
        "officePhone",     # call to office phone
        "securityQuestion" # security questions (disable for privileged accounts)
    )
    numberOfQuestionsToRegister = 5     # user registers 5 questions
    numberOfQuestionsRequired  = 3      # must answer 3 during reset
    customizeHelpdeskLink = $true
    customHelpdeskEmailOrUrl = "https://contoso.sharepoint.com/sites/IT/SitePages/Password-Help.aspx"
    customEmailAddressRestricted = $false
}

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/passwordResetPolicies" \
    -Body ($updateBody | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5)

# After changing the policy, verify the change took effect:
$updated = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/passwordResetPolicies"
Write-Host "SSPR type: $($updated.selfServicePasswordResetEnabledType)"
Write-Host "Methods required: $($updated.numberOfMethodsRequired)"
Write-Host "Allowed methods: $($updated.allowedMethods -join ', ')"
Security questions are the weakest SSPR method — answers can be guessed from social media or phishing. Disable them for privileged accounts and prefer Authenticator app or alternate email.
Account Unlock and Smart Lockout

SSPR optionally supports account unlock without a password reset — useful when an on-premises account is locked due to too many failed attempts but the user remembers their password. Account unlock requires Password Writeback and the Entra Connect service account to have the 'Unlock Account' delegated permission on AD DS.

Entra ID also protects cloud passwords with Smart Lockout, which operates independently of the on-premises lockout policy. Smart Lockout tracks failed attempts per password hash, distinguishing between familiar and unfamiliar locations. The default threshold is 10 attempts; configure it via directory settings.

# SSPR includes account unlock WITHOUT password reset — available when:
#   1. Password Writeback is enabled
#   2. The account is locked out in on-premises AD (not Entra)

# Check if a user's on-premises account is locked
Get-ADUser -Identity alice -Properties LockedOut, BadLogonCount, BadPasswordTime |
    Select-Object SamAccountName, LockedOut, BadLogonCount, BadPasswordTime

# Lock threshold policy (Fine-Grained Password Policy or Default Domain Policy):
Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy | Select-Object LockoutThreshold, LockoutDuration, LockoutObservationWindow

# Entra Smart Lockout protects cloud passwords independently:
# GET smart lockout configuration:
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/settings" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Where-Object { $_.displayName -eq "Password Rule Settings" } |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty values

# Smart Lockout settings:
$lockoutSettings = @{
    templateId = "5cf42378-d67d-4f36-ba46-e8b86229381d"
    values = @(
        @{ name = "LockoutThreshold"; value = "10" },      # 10 failed attempts = lockout
        @{ name = "LockoutDurationInSeconds"; value = "60" } # 60-second lockout window
    )
}
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/settings/<setting-id>" \
    -Body ($lockoutSettings | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 4)

# Unlock a cloud account (admin unlock, not SSPR):
# Revoke all sign-in sessions and clear smart lockout:
Invoke-MgUserRevokeSignInSession -UserId alice@contoso.com

# Force re-registration of SSPR methods for a user:
Update-MgUser -UserId alice@contoso.com \
    -AdditionalProperties @{ requireReregistrationOfMfaAuthenticationMethods = $true }
Lockout Type
Applies To
Configurable Via
Default Threshold
Entra Smart Lockout
Cloud passwords (PHS hash)
Directory settings API / admin center
10 attempts
AD DS Account Lockout Policy
On-premises AD account
Group Policy (Fine-Grained PP)
Org default (often 5–10)
SSPR Account Unlock
On-prem locked account
SSPR portal by user
N/A — user self-unlocks
Conditional Access Integration

SSPR and Conditional Access interact in important ways. When a user resets their password, Entra revokes all existing sessions, forcing re-authentication under current CA policies. This is intentional — it closes any sessions that an attacker might hold with the old credential. However, it also means users must sign in fresh after an SSPR reset.

Secure your SSPR deployment with complementary CA policies: block legacy authentication protocols (which cannot satisfy MFA), and alert when privileged accounts use SSPR (admins should have emergency access accounts, not rely on SSPR).

# SSPR + Conditional Access: Require MFA after SSPR to prevent account takeover
# When a user resets via SSPR, force re-authentication to validate the new credential

# 1. Revoke all existing sessions when SSPR is used (done automatically by Entra when
#    'Revoke sessions on password change' is enabled in tenant settings)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/identitySecurityDefaultsEnforcementPolicy"

# 2. CA policy: require MFA at next sign-in after password reset
# This is enforced by Entra automatically for accounts with enabled sign-in risk

# 3. CA policy: block legacy auth entirely so SSPR doesn't help attackers
#    who hold a legacy-auth token for the old password
$caParams = @{
    displayName = "Block Legacy Auth (supports SSPR security)"
    state = "enabled"
    conditions = @{
        clientAppTypes = @("exchangeActiveSync", "other")  # legacy protocols
        users = @{ includeUsers = @("All") }
    }
    grantControls = @{
        operator = "OR"
        builtInControls = @("block")
    }
}
New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -BodyParameter $caParams

# 4. Monitor: alert when SSPR is used for privileged accounts (admins should never
#    need SSPR if they have proper emergency access accounts)
$adminRoles = @(
    "Global Administrator",
    "Privileged Role Administrator",
    "Security Administrator"
)
# Alert in Sentinel using: AuditLogs
# | where Category == 'SelfServicePasswordManagement'
# | where Result == 'success'
# | where TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName in (admin_upn_list)
# | project TimeGenerated, TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName, OperationName
Enable 'Notify users on reset' AND 'Notify admins when admins reset passwords' in the SSPR policy — alerts catch unauthorized resets
If a user's SSPR-registered email is compromised, an attacker can use SSPR to take over the account — teach users to protect their alternate email
Consider requiring Temporary Access Pass (TAP) instead of SSPR for privileged accounts — TAP has a hard time limit and requires admin issuance
Sentinel rule: alert on SSPR success for accounts with active sign-in risk detections — this pattern indicates account takeover via SSPR
Troubleshooting Common SSPR Issues

Most SSPR failures fall into three categories: the user is not in scope (policy not enabled for them or not registered), Password Writeback is not configured for hybrid users, or the user's registered verification method is inaccessible. Use the registration details API to diagnose the first two.

# SSPR Troubleshooting Runbook

# 1. User says 'SSPR is not working' — check if they are in scope:
$user = Get-MgUser -UserId alice@contoso.com -Property "Id,DisplayName,OnPremisesSyncEnabled"
$regDetails = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/reports/authenticationMethods/userRegistrationDetails/$($user.Id)" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value
$regDetails | Select-Object isSsprRegistered, isSsprCapable, isSsprEnabled

# Common causes of isSsprEnabled = false:
#   a. User is not in the enabled group
#   b. SSPR scope is 'none'
#   c. User is a guest (SSPR doesn't support guests)

# 2. Password reset succeeds in cloud but old password works on-prem:
#    -> Password Writeback not configured, or Entra Connect sync is stalled
Get-ADSyncScheduler | Select-Object SyncCycleEnabled, NextSyncCyclePolicyType, CurrentlyRunning
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta

# 3. User can't receive the verification email:
#    -> Check their alternate email is set (NOT their work email):
$methods = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/alice@contoso.com/authentication/emailMethods" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value
$methods | Select-Object id, emailAddress

# 4. Locked account: SSPR can unlock on-premises if writeback is enabled
#    Verify the Azure AD Connect service account has 'Unlock Account' permissions:
dsacls "DC=contoso,DC=com" /I:S /G "CONTOSO\ADConnectSvc:CA;Reset Password;user"

# 5. Audit: find users who were blocked from SSPR due to suspicious activity:
$filter = [System.Uri]::EscapeDataString(
    "category eq 'SelfServicePasswordManagement' and result eq 'failure'"
)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/directoryAudits?" + '$filter=' + $filter + "&" + '$top=50' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Select-Object activityDateTime, activityDisplayName, \
        @{N="User";E={$_.targetResources[0].userPrincipalName}}, \
        @{N="FailReason";E={$_.additionalDetails | Where-Object key -eq "FailureReason" | Select-Object -ExpandProperty value}}
Symptom
Likely Cause
Fix
'Contact your admin' shown at SSPR portal
User not in SSPR scope or not registered
Add user to enabled group; trigger registration
Cloud reset works but on-prem credential unchanged
Password Writeback disabled or stalled
Enable writeback in Entra Connect; check sync scheduler
Verification code not received
Alternate email is wrong or not set
Update emailMethods via Graph; advise user to register
Account still locked after SSPR unlock
Service account lacks Unlock Account permission
Grant dsacls Unlock Account on user objects in AD
SSPR fails for synced user with 'technical problem'
Account not enabled in cloud
Check AccountEnabled attribute and assign license
Reporting on Registration Coverage and Enforcing Adoption

A deployed SSPR policy only reduces help desk load if users register before they forget their password. Entra ID exposes per-user registration details via the `userRegistrationDetails` API, allowing you to measure coverage, send automated nudges to unregistered users, and report progress to management.

Target: aim for ≥90% of SSPR-enabled users being 'capable' (registered with enough methods to satisfy the policy). Below 70%, SSPR will still generate significant help desk traffic from users who can't self-recover.

# Automated SSPR Registration Report with Enforcement # Goal: identify unregistered users, send them nudge emails, and track progress over time Connect-MgGraph -Scopes "Reports.Read.All", "Mail.Send", "User.Read.All" # Step 1: Get full registration status for all users $uri = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/reports/authenticationMethods/userRegistrationDetails" $allReg = @() do { $page = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET -Uri $uri $allReg += $page.value $uri = $page.'@odata.nextLink' } while ($uri) # Step 2: Compute summary stats $total = $allReg.Count $capable = ($allReg | Where-Object isSsprCapable -eq $true).Count $reg = ($allReg | Where-Object isSsprRegistered -eq $true).Count $eligible = ($allReg | Where-Object isSsprEnabled -eq $true).Count Write-Host ("{0} total | {1} enabled | {2} registered | {3} capable" -f $total, $eligible, $reg, $capable) Write-Host ("Registration rate (of enabled): {0:P0}" -f ($reg / [math]::Max($eligible,1))) # Step 3: Send reminder emails to unregistered-but-enabled users $nudgeTargets = $allReg | Where-Object { $_.isSsprEnabled -eq $true -and $_.isSsprRegistered -eq $false } foreach ($u in $nudgeTargets) { $body = @{ message = @{ subject = "Action Required: Register for Self-Service Password Reset" body = @{ contentType = "HTML" content = "

Dear user,

Please register at " + "aka.ms/ssprsetup " + "within 14 days to avoid being prompted at sign-in.

" } toRecipients = @(@{ emailAddress = @{ address = $u.userPrincipalName } }) } saveToSentItems = $false } Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \ -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/it-notifications@contoso.com/sendMail" \ -Body ($body | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 6) Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 100 # respect throttle } Write-Host "Sent nudge emails to $($nudgeTargets.Count) users." # Step 4: Write weekly report CSV $allReg | Select-Object userPrincipalName, isSsprEnabled, isSsprRegistered, isSsprCapable, \ isAdmin, isMfaRegistered, methodsRegistered | Export-Csv -Path ("C:\Reports\sspr-reg-$(Get-Date -f 'yyyyMMdd').csv") -NoTypeInformation
Schedule the report script as a weekly Azure Automation runbook with a Managed Identity — no stored credentials
Use a Conditional Access policy with 'Registration required' controls to prompt unregistered users during sign-in rather than relying on emails
Segment registration rates by department (via extensionAttribute) to identify teams that need targeted training
For newly on-boarded users, fire the nudge email on day 3 (not day 1, when users are overwhelmed with onboarding tasks)

Set 'Allow users to register when signing in' in the SSPR policy. This presents the combined security info registration to users at their next sign-in, collecting SSPR methods without requiring them to navigate to a separate portal.
Summary

Self-Service Password Reset lets users reset their Entra ID passwords independently by verifying their identity through pre-registered methods. Deploying SSPR requires: enabling the policy for the correct scope, driving user registration to completion, and — for hybrid environments — configuring Password Writeback on Entra Connect.
Scope SSPR to a pilot group first, validate registration rates, then expand to all users
Require 2 verification methods for higher-privilege accounts; 1 is acceptable for regular users
Enable Password Writeback for all hybrid tenants to prevent credential drift with on-premises AD
Monitor the SSPR Capable percentage and audit failed reset attempts via directoryAudits API
Disable security questions for admin accounts; prefer Authenticator app or email OTP
